seed_problem: gen emits pinned anchor nodes, deck load nodes, per-stratum rock ceilings and a steel ration; the contestant prints straight members; chk computes active (triangulated) members, integer member capacities, and the load ceiling as the deterministic min-cut of the force-path network; score = ceiling / canonical-ladder baseline.
mutation: composite: compose integer force-balance (flow conservation), a member-length budget, and a triangulation-topology activation rule into a single load-ceiling objective that is a min-cut over rock strata, so the winning strategy is to water-fill rationed steel into the weakest stratum rather than to add tonnage.
theme: bridge over a gorge from rationed steel
title: Gorge Bridge from Rationed Steel
innovation_hook: Maximum load is a min-cut-like property of force paths: sparse, correctly triangulated load paths crossing the weakest stratum beat dense meshes on strong strata, so quality comes from topology, not tonnage.
strategies: trivial: copy the single canonical triangulated ladder (F == baseline B, ratio 0.1)
greedy: build a full single-diagonal triangulated sheet, then X-brace panels in reading order until the ration runs out (blind to which stratum is the bottleneck)
strong: min-cut-aware water-filling -- from the base sheet, repeatedly add the X-brace that raises actual max-flow the most, pouring spare steel into exactly the weakest stratum
local search / LP-relaxation of the flow-cut allocation across strata under the steel budget
trap: The obvious 'more bracing = stronger' heuristic reinforces whichever stratum it reaches first (and dense meshing / long direct spans waste ration or hinge to zero); on the planted weak-stratum cases it lands far below the min-cut-aware builder.
